title: Sara Blakely Reveals Why Betting On Yourself Is The Ultimate Investment - YouTube
text:
Now, please welcome interviewer Moira Forbes, 
executive vice president Forbes, and Sarah  
Blakeley, founder Spanx and Sneaks. Hello 
everyone. The sunshine came out for you, Sarah.  
We also decided we would twin today um in terms of 
colors, feminine. Um we we planned these things uh  
far in advance. Um well Sarah I I I don't think 
there's uh anyone who better exemplifies the  
entrepreneurial spirit that drives so many women 
today but also the ways in which women are are  
paying that power forward than you. Um and I'll 
say at Forbes I feel really lucky because we've  
had a front row seat to your jour your journey. 
I think back to I think in 2001 was the first  
article uh uh that we did on Spanx when you you 
were filing your patent and going head-to-head  
with a potential competitor. And in 2012, Sarah 
was uh our our cover woman on uh our billionaire's  
list. Uh youngest female self-made billionaire. 
Only 10 self-made women were on this list. Fast  
forward to today, uh you're the founder of Sneaks. 
You're doing so many different projects. So if you  
were to have for I have a story about you just 
reminded me I forgot that I was in Forbes in  
2001 which was you know I was running Spanx out of 
my apartment in Atlanta. I had se pulling up with  
boxes of Spanx in this neighborhood and dro they 
drop them off at my apartment and a lot of times  
they leave them in front of the front door and 
then I have to go knock on the neighbors and say,  
"Can you help me move the boxes so I can get 
into my house and packing and shipping?" And  
I had been trying to get on QVC, which was 
interesting because Spanx was sold at Neiman  
Marcus and Burgdorf and all the very high-end 
retailers, and I wanted to be on QVC. And I had  
several people say, "Sarah, don't do that. You'll 
kill your brand if you do that." And I said, "No,  
I I'm not going to discount my price." They said, 
"Everyone discounts their price on QVC." That's  
what I learned what the V in QVC meant. When I 
met with them, the guy was like, "Do you know  
what the V stands for?" And I'm like, "Victor? did 
Victor start this company? He was like value and  
uh I said well and I I remember I talked to them 
about well you don't have a lot of products that  
are also sold at Neiman Marcus and I saw um QVC as 
an entrepreneur without a lot of money as my ad my  
free ad and I knew if I could be the one on camera 
explaining what this product was that it would  
be helpful. Where Forbes played in is I had been 
calling them and calling them and calling them and  
they they would not take me. They were not willing 
to put me on air. And then the Forbes article ran  
and I got a phone call out of the blue after being 
rejected for like a over a year and they said,  
"We're gonna give Spanx a chance." And I said 
to the same buyer who'd been saying no to me  
and I said, "Really? Why the change of heart?" 
And she said, "My boss saw the Forbes article."  
And I was like, "Well, she she had a very smart 
boss then." So thank you. Got my foot in the door  
at QVC. But it but it is it's it's 25 years 
and this extraordinary journey and and I'd  
be curious if if Forbes was writing a headline 
about you today, what would that headline be? Um
she's still in the game. I love that. And and 
and very much so. And I wanted to to dig into  
Spanx. How many women in this room are wearing 
shoes that in the end of the day they will do  
anything to take off, right? I got a solution 
for you with Spanx. I thought you were going to  
say how many people have Spanx on. Well, that 
then Yeah, I I'm I'm doubling down on on your  
I put my Spanx on in the car. I actually 
forgot my Spanx. This happens a lot. So,  
we just ran to Nordstrom. I'm like, the Spanx girl 
doesn't have her Spanx. And I bought the Spanx and  
I put them on the backseat of the car on the way 
here. I love that. Which is very interesting. Yes.  
I was going to say I don't know if anyone's ever 
done that. I don't think we would want to see that  
that happen this morning. Um it's kind of an art. 
It is an art, but it is uh it is something where  
you created an entirely new category. And it's 
so interesting with sneaks, you're going into one  
of the most established categories. What what 
did launching sneaks and and spanks, I think,  
really teach you about what it is to look at these 
industries still with outdated assumptions and  
outdated ways of reaching and meeting the needs of 
female consumers? Well, I for me everything starts  
with a problem and it's usually a problem that 
I'm experiencing. Um I couldn't find a solution of  
what to wear under white pants. I got frustrated 
with the undergarment options and then with sneaks  
obviously high heels are very uncomfortable 
and have been for like hundreds of years and  
I really wanted to take a new fresh approach 
at that. I think it's interesting that both of  
the industries that I decided to go in and try to 
disrupt were were really kind of on the decline.  
Like a lot of people I think chase what's the hot 
trend, where's everything happening right now and  
want to throw their hat in the ring there. I'm 
curious about industries that are dying or not  
doing well and why. And I think it was so clear 
that they just weren't listening to the consumer.  
The consumer's needs had changed. Girdles were 
not functional. They were hot and and weren't  
working. So for me to come along, all I did was 
take a material that had already been around for a  
long time with panty hoes and I I said that's what 
the undergarment needs to be made out of because  
it's like second skin and it's more breathable 
and it's not hot and bulky. Um, but the industry  
was on a double-digit decline. Like, no one was 
wearing panty hoes anymore and the the factories,  
but it made it also less competition for me to be 
going into a space that everybody was exiting and  
um really able to to see the why it's being like 
not successful and and turn it on its head. And  
right now like people stopped wearing high heels a 
lot of the times, right? So, I think if that I was  
in a business class, you know, somewhere, someone 
would probably say, "That's the last place you  
should be going." But I'm like, "No, that's kind 
of the first place I want to go because why?" And  
it's so clear to me. I mean, men have been making 
our high heels since like the 1500s. And they  
um haven't cared about how we feel, like really 
don't care. And I um I always ask for signs when  
I do things, especially if I make big decisions 
in my life. I always ask the universe for a sign.  
And I'd been saying publicly for 10 
years, I, you know, someone would say,  
'What's next for Sarah Blakeley? And I'd always 
say, I just want to invent a comfortable high  
heel and then retire. And I'd laugh. And after 
lobbing that out for 10 years to the universe,  
and no one did it. I one day was like, universe, 
am I supposed to do this? Is this why it hasn't  
happened yet? Give me a sign if if I am the one 
who's supposed to do this. And two days later,  
I turned on the Gail King in the morning. I 
was running out the door to Spanx and she was  
interviewing a shoe designer, which was really 
already weird for hard news, right? I was like,  
"This is a little unusual." And I just asked for 
the sign. And Gail says to the shoe designer on  
live television. You can Google it. You know, your 
shoe, she takes the shoe off. She holds it up. She  
goes, "Your shoe is a work of art, but I got to 
say, by the end of the day, I could be crippled."  
And I'm a product person who cares so deeply about 
how you feel. Like truly, I I care very much about  
how a woman feels and how we're moving through 
life. And I'm expecting this designer to go,  
I know, I know we're working on it. And he looked 
at Gail on TV and he goes, "I do not want my shoes  
to be comfortable." And her mouth was like, and 
my mouth was like, and she and then he said, "In  
fact, if someone says my shoes are comfortable, I 
take it as an insult." And that is the industry.  
It's very draconian. It took me 10 years. I flew 
over to Spain. I ultimately just moved the factory  
to Italy. And it it's been fascinating that all 
these years later because I started Spanx in 2000  
starting Sneaks, I got just as much rejection. 
Nobody wanted to help me make it. I got fired by  
like three factories after working with them for 
six months, one for eight months. They're like,  
"It's too challenging." The luxury shoe industry 
is fascinating how how it's done. It's like really  
not functional. And you know, either shoe com 
shoe companies were like, "We make luxury heels  
or we make a sneaker." No one was making both. 
And I set out to make a classic pump. That was  
my vision. I wanted it to, my first mandate, my 
creative mandate was in my mind, I want it to look  
exactly like our high heels, exactly what we're 
wearing. but exponentially com more comfortable.  
And I took two women off the bra team at Spanx 
because bras are pretty technical and I like  
I like working with people who have no idea how 
something's supposed to be done. And so the three  
of us had never made shoes before and we started 
making it. And four years Moira into it, I pulled  
the plug and I remember looking at the girls and 
I said, "We didn't get it over the finish line.  
We've really tried." And they were crying and they 
said, "Sarah, we like these heels better than any  
other heel." And I said, "I agree, but it's not 
exponentially better." And I really believe if  
you're going to start a company or start go into 
a new category, your product should be 10x better  
than what's already out there. So I have this sort 
of 10x idea in my mind. And it wasn't exponential  
enough. So I as I was leaving the factory, I 
just looked at the factory owner and I was like,  
"Why has this been so hard?" Like I just want my 
high heel to feel as comfortable as my sneaker.  
Like we put a man on the moon, now we've shot 
a go-kart to Mars. We're driving it by remote  
control from Earth. Like, why has this been 
so hard? And as soon as I said that out loud,  
I looked at the two girls on my team and I was 
like, "Oh, wait." And they were like, "Sarah,  
no." Sarah, like, "No." And I was like, "Hold on 
a minute." And I went home back to Atlanta and  
I cut my high heel in half and I cut my sneaker 
in half and I glued them together. And I walked  
around my house for three days in that shoe, 
mostly in my pajamas. And my kids were like  
really young and going, "What is mom doing?" And I 
was just daydreaming like, "Could this even work?  
Could I take sneaker properties?" But that's the 
interesting thing about innovation. This is not  
what I set out to make, but I pivoted and kind of 
let the goal of the world's most comfortable high  
heel dictate what it was going to end up being. 
I'm I'm so struck in terms of the similarities of  
the experiences. You said, "Do you still face 
the same rejection?" I I think most of us are  
really surprised by that sort of given what you've 
built, your brand, your reputation, and the fact  
that you sort of physically made the product for 
yourself in the same way you did with Spanx. Um,  
just sort of get getting into the weed of it. 
Was there anything though that you had to learn  
or unlearn through this process of going into a 
different category, a different stage in life?
um anything I had to learn or unlearn from Spanx 
moving into this in terms of just the difference  
what the differences were in terms of that type of 
entrepreneurial journey what stayed the same and  
and what felt new and different I mean one of the 
biggest differences is I had children so I didn't  
have children when I started Spanx and really 
when I made a pro and con a pro and con column  
for creating this having children watch me start 
something was in a big reason why I did sneaks  
because my children didn't get to see me start 
Spanx. They didn't see the grind. They're kind of  
seeing the aftermath of Spanx. And so seeing the 
ups and downs and me, you know, working so hard  
and getting rejected, that was really important 
to me for them to see. I mean, everything's  
different. When I started Spanx, like the internet 
was just becoming a thing. Was like, are you going  
to sell on www. I'm like, I don't know, maybe like 
okay, let's try this. I mean, so everything was so  
different. Um, and it's fascinating to me now 
because I stood in department stores for seven  
hours a day for years um, selling Spanx for the 
department stores. And so to launch sneaks and  
digitally, it's kind of out there very quickly, 
that's that's been very different. I think it's  
been fascinating that I experienced as much 
rejection the second time around. I think  
that it was um you know what was different was 
the first time around the rejection um felt more  
expected and this time around it didn't. So it was 
more humbling. How did you react to that? Um do  
you know who I am? No, I'm kidding. They did not 
care. Um I No, I I was I cried, you know, I cried  
and I I got frustrated and I started and stopped 
the process many times. But ultimately, my goal  
was like, I just want this product for myself. 
And if I want it for myself, there's going to be  
other women out there that feel good in this. And 
that's really what I love. I love to make women  
feel good. I love to make them feel good. And 
that's been such a throughine of of your career,  
not just being a female founder, but the ways in 
which you pay that back in terms of supporting  
so many other female entrepreneurs, doing so much 
to really empower uh women in extraordinary ways.  
When we were in the green room earlier, Sarah 
and I were saying in the age of AI, the different  
types of leadership qualities um that now may be 
more foundational, more at the maybe more needed  
now more than ever. Can you share um um what 
you talk about in terms of the divine feminine  
rising and why you were allowed to take that 
leadership approach in building Spanx? Yeah,  
I we were talking about it. I I feel business is 
a male construct. Okay. Men created business. They  
defined what it is. They defined what success 
looks like. They made all the rules. There  
were not women at all involved in the creation of 
business and what business is and how it runs. We  
have only really been allowed to play in this 
arena in a meaningful way in like the last 30  
years, 35 years, not that long. And the feminine 
has been very squashed in this construct. The  
feminine was made to look as a weakness. You know, 
it was even the men because I think masculine and  
feminine is energy. It doesn't have to just be 
gender. So, I even think the men in the workplace  
were meant to suppress the feminine because it was 
made to seem as a weakness. I saw the feminine as  
a superpower. And when I started Spanx, two 
guys came up to me in Atlanta. They saw me in  
the paper, the AJC, and they said, "Sarah, we we 
read that you invented something." And I said,  
"Yes, I'm so excited." I was 29 years old. And 
they go, "Yeah, good luck. Business is war." And  
the guy patted me on the shoulder. And the other 
guy started laughing. He goes, "Yep, business is  
war. I hope you're ready to go to war." And I went 
home to my apartment. I sat on the floor and I  
was like, I don't want to go to war. Why would I 
want to go to war? And I just made this conscious  
decision. I'm going to do this very differently. 
I'm going to honor my intuition. I'm going to be  
kind. I'm going to try to solve problems for 
people out there and make their life better  
and not try to annihilate the competition and 
focus like I never even paid any attention to my  
competition. I just paid attention to my consumer. 
And um I feel really proud of that because I felt  
like I was sort of being a case study that um like 
how will this turn out? I didn't even know if it  
was going to work. But I always choose in my whole 
journey I chose intuition over data. And um I was  
saying you know I used a lot of vulnerability as a 
leader and also as the face of the brand. I mean,  
I went and took a photograph, you guys, of myself 
in and out of Spanx in white pants. And in the  
before picture, it was not pretty. You know, there 
was like cellulite and a panty line. And I'd stand  
there with a laminated picture of my own butt 
and be like, "This is me." And everyone's like,  
"Huh?" And I I remember Pete Nordstrom after a 
morning meeting, he came over to me. He's like,  
"Can you do you is there anyone else in the 
company that could be your before picture? Does  
it have to be you?" And I'm like, "It should 
be me." I'm like, this is the reason that I'm  
connecting with the consumer. But um anyway, I was 
saying that I didn't have investors and I never  
took outside investors. So I started Spanx with 
$5,000 from selling fax machines doortodoor for  
seven years in Florida. And um then I self-funded 
from that. And I think that that was also part  
of my experiment. It really allowed me to stay 
connected to that divine feminine. Still today,  
as you mentioned, sort of that it's still a male 
construct in business. We're seeing openings and  
opportunities and cracks, but very much so, uh, 
very similar dynamics. What advice would you give  
to female entrepreneurs who are navigating this 
landscape and still getting mixed feedback very  
much so in rooms investors and the like around 
their leadership approach and using those types of  
things within their businesses. Yeah. I mean I I 
would say lean into your superpower. I think a a a  
female's intuition is so magical and powerful and 
I I always say where is your intuition loudest and  
I like to talk about where does your mind wander 
because I think that's when we get downloads and  
for me my mind wanders when I'm driving in the car 
and once I identified that I take time in my car  
to go on what my friends call my fake commute I 
would live six minutes from Spanx but I would get  
in my car and drive one hour aimlessly around 
town before I got to the office because I knew  
I would have really creative and good thinking 
around that. But I would tell them lean into the  
the superpowers of what what makes us, you know, 
unique. Um and um for me, I left corporate America  
and bet on myself because I found at least in 
the year 1998, it was very difficult to to really  
honor that in that space. when you look at your 
work, you've you've do done so many things that  
are firsts just in in terms of your business, but 
also the way that you you've approached your brand  
um and and the like. Do you see yourself as as 
someone who has chased firsts or is it someone  
who has just not accepted the limits that that 
existed? I think the latter. I'm always asking  
why in everything. It's like why is it like this? 
Why? Why? Why? How long has it been this way? Why  
hasn't it? who was the first to think of this? 
Could it be better? So, I think it's more about  
not accepting the status quo. And you know, I want 
to just mention I've been working on my mindset  
since I'm 16 years old. I started listening to 
tapes. I'm dating myself uh by Dr. Wayne Dyer  
and it was how to be a no limit person. But it was 
really talking about visualizing what you want,  
manifesting it, law of attraction, not fearing 
failure, not caring what other people think about  
you. And um that is really important especially 
as women for us to find that confidence because  
if you are going to say I want to buck the 
whole system. That's scary. You have to have  
a lot of inner strength and a and a very strong 
inner compass to be able to stand in that space.  
And society has not really made it that um you 
know we haven't really nourished that in women  
to stand in a space and go this isn't right like 
maybe none of this is right and maybe I'm the one  
that has the answer and especially if you're 
not the expert like I haven't been the expert  
in anything that I've done but I've stood in 
the confidence and part of it is because of  
how much I've worked on my mindset. It's been so 
important to me to to do that. We we spent a lot  
of time getting stimula from everywhere. So, I was 
like, I really want a portion of what I listen to  
every day to be something telling me that I'm 
unstoppable and why not me and I can do it.  
I love that. That intention, that intention and 
being unstoppable. Well, Sarah, congratulations  
on on this new chapter. I can honestly say I 
can walk miles in these shoes. Um, you you 10x,  
20x, 30x, I don't know what the X's are, but you 
really have changed the category. and also given  
so much inspiration that that all of us can have 
different chapters and bring those experiences to  
bear in new ways. So, thank you so much. Thank 
you. Thank you everyone. Thank you. [Music]
